2605.18562.title: Estimating Item Difficulty with Large Language Models as Experts
2605.18562.paper_summary: Accurate estimates of item difficulty are essential for valid assessment and effective adaptive learning. However, for newly created tasks, response data are typically unavailable. Pretesting and expert judgement can be costly and slow, while machine learning methods often require large labelled training datasets. Recent work suggests that large language models (LLMs) may help. However, there is limited evidence on the elicitation procedures and prompt configurations used to emulate experts for difficulty estimation. This study addresses this gap by evaluating three off-the-shelf LLMs as difficulty raters for newly created items without access to response data. Using an item bank from an online learning system, the study examined 6 domains of primary-school mathematics, with empirical difficulty estimates treated as empirical reference. The study used a full factorial design crossing three factors: judgement format (absolute vs pairwise), decision type (hard decisions vs token-probability-based estimates), and prompting strategy (zero-shot vs few-shot). LLM-derived difficulty estimates were compared with empirical difficulties using Spearman rank correlations. Across domains, LLM-based estimates exhibited moderate to strong positive correlations with empirical item difficulties. For simpler arithmetic tasks, some configurations approached the upper end of the accuracy range reported for human experts in previous research. Pairwise comparison consistently outperformed absolute judgement in the absence of additional refinements. However, when token-level probabilities were incorporated and examples of items with known empirical difficulty were provided, the absolute judgement configuration likewise demonstrated moderate-to-high alignment. The study positions LLMs as a promising tool for initial item calibration and offers insights into effective workflow configuration.
2605.18562.paper_summary_zh: 準確的題目難度估計對於有效的評估和適應性學習至關重要。<lb/>然而，對於新創建的任務，回應數據通常是不可用的。<lb/>預測測試和專家判斷可能成本高昂且耗時，而機器學習方法通常需要大量標記的訓練數據集。<lb/>最近的研究表明，大型語言模型（LLMs）可能有所幫助。<lb/>然而，關於模仿專家進行難度估計的引導程序和提示配置的證據有限。<lb/>本研究通過評估三種現成的LLM作為新創建項目的難度評估者，來填補這一空白，且無需訪問回應數據。<lb/>使用來自在線學習系統的題庫，本研究檢查了6個小學數學領域，並將經驗難度估計視為經驗參考。<lb/>本研究使用了全因子設計，交叉了三個因素：判斷格式（絕對與成對）、決策類型（硬決策與基於標記概率的估計）和提示策略（零樣本與少樣本）。<lb/>LLM衍生的難度估計與經驗難度使用斯皮爾曼等級相關進行比較。<lb/>在各個領域中，基於LLM的估計顯示出與經驗題目難度的中等到強正相關。<lb/>對於較簡單的算術任務，一些配置接近於先前研究中報告的人類專家的準確性範圍的上限。<lb/>在缺乏額外改進的情況下，成對比較始終優於絕對判斷。<lb/>然而，當納入標記級概率並提供已知經驗難度的題目示例時，絕對判斷配置同樣顯示出中到高的一致性。<lb/>本研究將LLM定位為初步題目校準的有前景工具，並提供了有效工作流程配置的見解。
2605.18562.author: Diana Kolesnikova et.al.
2605.18562.authors: Diana Kolesnikova, Kirill Fedyanin, Abe D. Hofman, Matthieu J. S. Brinkhuis, Maria Bolsinova
